articles.question_title: How should SMEs decide whether an AI copilot is ready for real operations?
articles.summary: SMEs can evaluate AI copilots through small workflow trials, role-based review steps, and measurable time-saving targets before expanding usage across teams.
articles.why_it_matters: AI adoption is shifting from experimentation to operational discipline, where teams need clear rules for quality, review, and responsibility.
articles.business_angle: A practical rollout can begin with low-risk tasks such as draft generation, meeting note cleanup, FAQ support, and internal knowledge search.
articles.ai_pm_angle: AI PM learners should define the user workflow, success metric, human review point, and failure mode before recommending a broader rollout.
articles.risk_note: Teams may overtrust generated answers if they skip source checking, access control, or staff training.

articles.question_title: Why should AI product teams treat evaluation as part of the product, not an afterthought?
articles.summary: AI products need practical evaluation loops that test answer quality, user trust, edge cases, and human review behavior rather than only relying on model capability claims.
articles.why_it_matters: Evaluation design helps teams separate impressive demos from product features that can be safely used in real workflows.
articles.business_angle: A lightweight evaluation checklist can reduce wasted budget by showing whether an AI feature improves speed, quality, or decision confidence.
articles.ai_pm_angle: This is a core AI PM skill: convert vague model potential into measurable product acceptance criteria.
articles.risk_note: Without evaluation criteria, teams may ship features that look useful in demos but fail under real user tasks.

articles.question_title: How can education and NGO teams use AI without building a full custom platform first?
articles.summary: Education and NGO teams can start with structured prompts, reviewed summaries, and reusable templates before investing in custom systems or automated pipelines.
articles.why_it_matters: Small mission-driven teams often need reliable operational improvement more than complex AI infrastructure.
articles.business_angle: Use cases such as report summarization, stakeholder updates, programme reflection, and learning-material drafting can be tested with low setup cost.
articles.ai_pm_angle: The product opportunity is to design safe workflows around existing tools, including review ownership, source attribution, and repeatable templates.
articles.risk_note: Sensitive beneficiary data should not be pasted into public AI tools without clear data handling rules and organizational approval.

articles.question_title: What governance habits should teams add before scaling AI-assisted decisions?
articles.summary: Teams should document intended use, human oversight, risk level, source handling, and escalation paths before AI outputs influence decisions or public communication.
articles.why_it_matters: Governance becomes easier when it is built into daily workflow design rather than added after problems appear.
articles.business_angle: A simple risk checklist can help managers decide which use cases are safe for automation, which require review, and which should stay manual.
articles.ai_pm_angle: AI PMs can turn governance into product requirements: permissions, disclaimers, review states, audit logs, and rollback plans.
articles.risk_note: Overly abstract governance documents may fail if frontline users do not have practical examples and clear responsibility boundaries.

articles.question_title: Why do AI applications need security thinking even when the first release is only an MVP?
articles.summary: AI MVPs still need boundaries for prompt injection, unsafe tool use, data exposure, and unreviewed automation, especially when they connect to files or external services.
articles.why_it_matters: Early prototypes often become real workflows, so weak safety assumptions can become long-term operational risks.
articles.business_angle: Teams can keep cost low while still adding basic controls such as scoped access, approval gates, validation scripts, and audit notes.
articles.ai_pm_angle: This supports a product mindset where safety is part of the workflow design, not a separate engineering afterthought.
articles.risk_note: Connecting AI agents to files, email, or terminals without scope limits can increase the chance of accidental data loss or unsafe execution.

articles.question_title: How can a static AI content product stay useful before adding automation?
articles.summary: A static radar can remain useful if it offers clear categories, concise business angles, source links, and a repeatable publishing workflow.
articles.why_it_matters: This lets teams validate audience value before spending effort on scraping, accounts, personalization, or backend infrastructure.
articles.business_angle: A static MVP is suitable for portfolio validation, stakeholder demos, and early content-market fit testing.
articles.ai_pm_angle: AI PM learners can use this pattern to show scope control, information architecture, and content QA without overbuilding the technical stack.
articles.risk_note: The main bottleneck is editorial consistency; without a repeatable content process, updates can stop even if the site works technically.
